explanation:
**Construction** requires precise technical calculations: concrete, steel reinforcement, masonry, plaster, and waterproofing. Standards such as ACI and ASTM provide guidance for material selection and design methods.

## How It's Calculated

This calculator determines concrete volume for continuous footings using your input values for length, width, and depth. It applies the formula directly and alerts you if values fall outside typical ranges.

## Important Notes

This calculation is for reference only. For critical decisions—structural design, financial commitments, or legal matters—consult a licensed structural engineer or building professional. Values are current through 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.a: A continuous footing (strip footing) is a long, narrow foundation supporting a linear load such as a wall or row of columns. It distributes weight evenly across the soil below.

faq.q: How deep should a continuous footing be?
faq.a: Footings must extend below the frost line in your region. A typical minimum depth is 50 cm (20 inches). Always consult local building codes and a geotechnical engineer.

faq.q: What concrete mix is typical for continuous footings?
faq.a: Standard mix: strength class H21 or equivalent (approximately 350 kg cement per m³, ratio 1:2:3 cement:sand:stone) for typical structures. Verify with local standards and your engineer.

faq.q: How much rebar do continuous footings need?
faq.a: Typical design: 4–6 reinforcement bars (diameter 8–10 mm) with stirrups at specified spacing per building code. Size and spacing depend on loads and local requirements.

faq.q: When can I remove the forms?
faq.a: Minimum 7 days of moist curing is required. Forms can typically be removed after 7 days, but full strength develops over 28 days. Follow local codes.
